# Evaluating the Agentic Architect {#sec-evaluating-agentic-architect}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Measurement is the first step that leads to control and eventually to improvement. If you can’t measure something, you can’t understand it. If you can’t understand it, you can’t control it. If you can’t control it, you can’t improve it."*
>
> — H. James Harrington
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** H. James Harrington, an author and quality engineer, emphasized that systemic improvement requires rigorous, well-defined measurement. In AI-assisted hardware design, the immediate temptation is to measure the final instructions-per-cycle (IPC) of the generated chip. But this conflates a lucky guess with systemic engineering capability. To build reliable systems, we must measure the *agent*, not just the *artifact*.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Preceding chapters focused on verifying the architectural designs generated by an AI and running bounded studies to prove their value. But as the industry adopts Architecture 2.0, a parallel requirement emerges: evaluating and benchmarking the agent itself. Software engineering relies on standardized evaluations like SWE-bench and ToolBench to test agents on realistic workflows. Computer architecture, with its vast state spaces and expensive simulators, requires its own specialized suite of evaluations. This chapter defines the fundamental, timeless metrics required to rank agentic architects—pass-rate, trajectory efficiency, autonomous recovery, tool precision, and context retention—and explores the underlying philosophy of why we must measure the process of design, not just the outcome.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
Why is measuring the final performance of an AI-generated chip insufficient, and how do we rigorously evaluate the capability, efficiency, and reliability of the agent driving the design loop?
:::

## Why Architects Measure

Before we can benchmark an AI architect, we must understand why human architects measure anything at all. Computer architecture is a strictly empirical discipline. We do not evaluate a processor simply by reading its source code; we evaluate it by running standardized workloads (like SPEC CPU or MLPerf) and measuring its physical consequences (IPC, power, area, and latency). Because building a physical chip costs millions of dollars and takes years, we measure these properties early and often using simulators and analytical models (like the Roofline model or McPAT). The goal of traditional architectural measurement is risk reduction—we measure to guarantee that a design point will meet its physical requirements before we commit it to silicon.

When an AI agent enters the design loop, the object of measurement fundamentally shifts. We are no longer just evaluating the *artifact* (the generated chip); we must now evaluate the *agent* (the generative model driving the loop).

## The Shift: From Artifact to Agent

Evaluating an agentic architect is tricky because an AI can arrive at a correct architecture through entirely incorrect, unscalable means. In traditional software engineering, execution is cheap. An agent can write a Python script, compile it, and run a unit test in ten milliseconds. Therefore, software benchmarks often judge agents simply by their final output. Did the unit test pass? If yes, the agent succeeds.

In hardware, generation is cheap, but execution (verification and simulation) is prohibitively expensive. If we only measure the final outcome (e.g., "The agent improved IPC by 5%"), we are entirely blind to *how* it achieved that outcome. Did it reason causally about the pipeline, or did it act like a stochastic monkey, mutating Verilog blindly until the simulator accidentally returned a passing grade? In software, a monkey that eventually types a working script is amusing. In hardware, a monkey that burns 10,000 hours of cycle-accurate simulation compute to find a 5% gain destroys tape-out schedules and computing budgets.

@tbl-eval-shift summarizes this fundamental paradigm shift.

| **Dimension** | **Evaluating the Artifact (Traditional)** | **Evaluating the Agent (Architecture 2.0)** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **What is measured?** | The physical consequences of the processor. | The algorithmic efficiency of the search. |
| **Standard Benchmarks** | SPEC CPU, MLPerf, PARSEC. | ArchEval, SWE-bench, ToolBench. |
| **Primary Cost** | Paid once per design phase (NRE). | Paid iteratively during every loop (Simulator Tax). |
| **Failure Mode** | Thermal throttling, timing violations. | Hallucination loops, API misuse, context amnesia. |
| **Goal of Measurement** | Prove the chip works. | Prove the agent can reason causally. |

: **The Paradigm Shift in Measurement.** Evaluating an AI architect requires an entirely different set of metrics than evaluating the chip it produces. {#tbl-eval-shift tbl-colwidths="[20,40,40]"}

Evaluating the agent means evaluating the *process* of design. By pulling from latent machine learning concepts—such as reward hacking, epistemic uncertainty, and the credit assignment problem—we can ground exactly what we must measure and why.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter, you can define the core metrics required to evaluate an agentic architect. Specifically, you can:

- **Understand** the "Simulator Tax" and why trajectory efficiency is more important than final performance;
- **Measure** an agent's pass-rate and adherence to environment bounds to prevent reward hacking;
- **Assess** an agent's ability to autonomously recover from error logs by resolving epistemic uncertainty;
- **Evaluate** tool-use precision and long-horizon constraint retention to solve the credit assignment problem.
:::

## Lessons from Software Engineering Benchmarks

To establish a baseline for how to benchmark agents, we can look to software engineering. The machine learning community has poured immense resources into evaluating code generation. Benchmarks like **HumanEval** measure whether a model can generate a single, functional Python snippet. More advanced frameworks like **SWE-bench** [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench] evaluate whether an agent can clone a real-world repository, parse a GitHub issue, and autonomously submit a patch that passes the project's hidden test suite.

What we learn from software benchmarks is the importance of *execution-based evaluation*. It is not enough to ask an LLM multiple-choice questions about Verilog syntax; the agent must physically execute the code in a sandbox. However, software benchmarks have a critical blind spot when imported into hardware design: they are almost entirely indifferent to **execution cost**.

In SWE-bench, if an agent runs `pytest` 500 times in a row, iterating through brute-force print statements, the evaluation framework does not penalize the agent, as long as it eventually submits a working patch. Running 500 Python unit tests takes thirty seconds. Running 500 cycle-accurate gem5 simulations takes thirty days. Therefore, the core lesson from software is that we must evaluate agents in realistic, sandboxed execution environments, but we must *deviate* from software by making **search efficiency** a primary metric of success, rather than just functional correctness.

## The Core Agentic Metrics

If we cannot simply port SWE-bench into computer architecture, what exactly should we measure? Evaluating the process of design requires tracking metrics across the three core AI roles introduced in @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization: **Generation** (creating the design), **Prediction** (estimating its value), and **Optimization** (searching the space).

@tbl-agent-metrics provides the framework for what must be measured, which AI role it evaluates, and the underlying failure modes each metric is designed to expose.

| **Metric** | **AI Role** | **What it Measures** | **Why we Measure it** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Pass-Rate ($Pass@K$)** | Generation | The percentage of proposals that compile without errors. | Defeats Goodhart's Law; prevents reward hacking with illegal topologies. |
| **Proxy Calibration** | Prediction | The correlation between the fast analytical proxy and the slow ground-truth simulator. | Exposes the "Proxy Mirage"; ensures the agent's internal compass points true north. |
| **Trajectory Efficiency (AUC)** | Optimization | The ratio of environment interactions to the quality of the final reward. | Exposes the Simulator Tax; proves the search algorithm is methodical rather than stochastic. |
| **Self-Correction (SCR)** | Generation & Optimization | The probability that an agent successfully resolves a failing error log on its next attempt. | Evaluates how well the agent resolves epistemic uncertainty. |
| **Context Retention Length** | Generation | The maximum number of conversational turns the agent can endure before forgetting constraints. | Exposes the Credit Assignment problem; tests if the agent remembers *why* it made a change. |

: **Core Metrics for Evaluating the Agentic Architect.** A rigorous evaluation must map its metrics to the distinct roles of generation, prediction, and optimization. {#tbl-agent-metrics tbl-colwidths="[20,15,30,35]"}

The following sections dissect each of these metrics, grounding them in the latent machine learning theories that make them necessary.

## Defeating Reward Hacking with Bounds Adherence

The most basic evaluation of an agent is its ability to produce a syntactically and semantically valid proposal. In an open-topology search, generative models will frequently propose illegal configurations. An agent might attempt to route a memory bus through a blocked macro, request an L2 cache size that violates the SRAM area budget, or output Verilog with syntax errors.

**What to measure:** The zero-shot pass rate (success on the first try) versus the $k$-shot pass rate. In software, $Pass@K$ is calculated by sampling $c$ candidates to see if at least one passes. In architecture, a "pass" means the output successfully clears the first layer of validation (e.g., passing a Pydantic JSON schema check or compiling without errors under `verilator --lint-only`).

**Why we measure it (Goodhart's Law & Reward Hacking):** In machine learning, Goodhart's Law states that when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. If we only reward an agent for maximizing IPC, the agent will learn to "reward hack." It will propose impossible chips—like a cache size larger than the die—because the analytical model mathematically predicts a massive IPC boost. Measuring bounds adherence proves the agent is actually engineering within physical reality, rather than exploiting the abstractions of the simulator.

## Proxy Calibration for Prediction

Before an agent queries the slow simulator, it usually queries a fast surrogate model (a proxy) to predict the outcome. Evaluating the prediction role means measuring how much we can trust this proxy.

**What to measure:** The Proxy Calibration Margin. This is the statistical correlation (e.g., Pearson or Spearman rank correlation) between the surrogate model's predicted reward and the ground-truth simulator's actual reward across a wide distribution of design points.

**Why we measure it (The Proxy Mirage):** If a proxy model is poorly calibrated, the optimization algorithm will confidently navigate toward a "Proxy Mirage"—a region of the design space where the proxy predicts massive performance gains, but the actual simulator returns terrible results. Measuring calibration ensures that the agent's internal compass points toward true north, preventing the search algorithm from wasting time chasing ghosts.

## Trajectory Efficiency and Simulation Costs

An agent's true value is determined by its trajectory efficiency—how quickly it converges on an optimal design without wandering blindly.

**What to measure:** Instead of just recording the final score, we must measure the Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the agent's trajectory. Plot the number of environment roundtrips (simulator calls) on the X-axis against the best-seen reward on the Y-axis. An efficient agent will have a steep initial slope, converging on the target in very few steps. Additionally, teams must report the "Simulation-to-Inference Ratio"—the time spent waiting on the environment versus the time spent generating code.

**Why we measure it (The Simulator Tax):** In AI alignment, researchers talk about the "alignment tax"—the compute overhead required to make a model safe. In computer architecture, there is a **Simulator Tax**. Every time an agent makes an unreasoned guess, it incurs a massive Simulator Tax. If Agent A finds a 5% performance improvement in 10 simulation cycles, and Agent B finds a 6% improvement but requires 1,000 simulation cycles, Agent A is vastly superior for practical deployment. Evals must penalize the Simulator Tax, heavily favoring agents that rely on reasoned, methodical exploration over brute-force generation.

## Resolving Epistemic Uncertainty via Autonomous Recovery

When an architecture environment returns an error—such as a setup time violation from OpenSTA or a routing congestion map—the agent must parse that complex output and adjust its next proposal.

**What to measure:** We measure the Self-Correction Rate (SCR):
$$ SCR = \frac{\text{Resolved Errors}}{\text{Total Environment Failures}} $$
Additionally, we must track the "Looping Metric": the number of times an agent proposes the *exact same* failing design state repeatedly because it failed to digest the error feedback.

**Why we measure it (Epistemic vs. Aleatoric Uncertainty):** In statistics, epistemic uncertainty is uncertainty that can be reduced with more information and reasoning, while aleatoric uncertainty is inherent, irreducible noise. An error log from a synthesis tool contains deep, causal information (epistemic). A strong agent reads the log, reduces its uncertainty, and causally fixes the missing semicolon or routing congestion. A weak agent treats the error log as aleatoric noise—it ignores the text and just makes another random guess. The Self-Correction Rate explicitly measures the agent's capacity for causal reasoning.

## Tool-Use Precision and Credit Assignment

Modern agents operate by calling tools—invoking a synthesis script, querying a specific timing path, or plotting a power map.

**What to measure:** The "Hallucinated Tool Call" rate (attempting to call tools that don't exist in the environment schema) and the "Parameter Error" rate (passing strings instead of floats, or requesting paths that don't exist).

**Why we measure it (The Credit Assignment Problem):** In reinforcement learning, the credit assignment problem asks: if an agent takes 1000 steps and achieves a massive reward at the end, which specific step was responsible for the success? If an AI designs a whole SoC and achieves a 10% speedup, was it the cache modification, the ALU change, or the interconnect routing? If the agent guesses blindly, neither the agent nor the human architect can assign credit to the correct mechanism. By measuring how precisely an agent invokes telemetry tools (e.g., explicitly querying the critical path using STA before modifying the ALU), we prove that the agent knows *why* a design works. It solves the credit assignment problem in real-time, making its architectural choices explainable and defensible.

## Context Retention for Long-Horizon Planning

Hardware design is not a single-turn chat prompt; it is a multi-week, stateful project. An agent might learn a crucial insight about the memory subsystem's latency limits on Day 1, which it must remember and apply when fixing a pipeline deadlock on Day 14.

**What to measure:** Long-horizon benchmarks must explicitly inject a constraint early in the context window (e.g., "Do not exceed a 10mW power limit on the ALU") and track whether the agent violates that constraint dozens of simulation turns later. This is the architectural equivalent of the "Needle-In-A-Haystack" evaluation.

**Why we measure it:** As context windows grow to millions of tokens, models often suffer from amnesia, where they forget earlier instructions or silently drop constraints as the log history expands. Measuring long-horizon contextual awareness ensures the agent can manage complex engineering projects. The Artificial Analysis Briefcase explicitly tests this by grading deliverables that rely on context established weeks prior in the simulation; computer architecture benchmarks must do exactly the same for deep RTL state.

## The Jevons Paradox of Agentic Tool Use

As we evaluate these agents, we must confront a fundamental trade-off in computational complexity: the cost of the agent's inference versus the cost of the environment's simulation.

Training a foundational architecture model (e.g., NVIDIA's ChipNeMo [@LiuEtAl2023ChipNeMo]) requires a massive, one-time Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost. However, *running* that model as an autonomous agent inside a design loop incurs a recurring inference cost. In traditional EDA, the simulator was the undisputed computational bottleneck. But as agents scale to hundreds of billions of parameters, the $O(N^2)$ complexity of transformer inference begins to aggressively compete with the simulator's cost.

This introduces a phenomenon akin to the Jevons Paradox (@fig-jevons-paradox). The Jevons Paradox states that as technological efficiency increases, resource consumption increases due to rising demand. In Architecture 2.0, as simulation tools become faster and more accessible via clean APIs, the agent simply queries them *more often*.

Furthermore, there is a strict inverse relationship between an agent's intrinsic capability and its reliance on external tools. A highly capable agent can reason causally and only queries the simulator to verify its final answer. A weak agent relies entirely on the simulator, querying it thousands of times to find a working configuration through stochastic trial and error.

![The Economics of Agentic Evaluation. The total computational cost of evaluating an agent forms a U-shaped curve. Weak agents incur a massive Simulator Tax through brute-force interactions. Frontier agents minimize simulation but incur high inference costs. Evaluation benchmarks must capture the total sum of this trade-off.](images/F9-compute-economics.pdf){#fig-jevons-paradox fig-alt="A U-shaped curve showing that total evaluation compute is a trade-off between inference cost and simulation cost." width=80%}

When benchmarking agents, we must measure this total computational complexity. A benchmark that ignores the inference cost of the agent or the simulation cost of the environment fails to capture the true economics of the Agentic Design Space Exploration loop.

## Conclusion and Carry Forward {#sec-conclusion-and-carry-forward}

By standardizing these metrics into a formal evaluation suite, hardware organizations can objectively rank competing models. More importantly, they establish a baseline for what must be reported in architectural AI research. By understanding the deep ML philosophies underlying these metrics—Goodhart's Law, the Simulator Tax, Epistemic Uncertainty, and Credit Assignment—we ensure that adopted algorithmic agents are truly capable of navigating the complex, highly constrained world of computer architecture.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Evaluating the agent is entirely distinct from evaluating the hardware. Always measure pass-rate ($Pass@K$), trajectory efficiency (AUC), autonomous recovery ($SCR$), tool precision, and long-horizon context retention.
- **Reader test:** Can you explain why an agent that treats simulator error logs as aleatoric noise (randomness) instead of epistemic uncertainty will inevitably incur a massive Simulator Tax?
- **Up next:** @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack examines how these evaluated agents are deployed and how study structures change across different layers of the hardware stack, from microarchitecture to physical design.
:::
